name: reasoning-inductive
description: Extract patterns and generalizations from multiple observations. Use when detecting recurring themes, building predictive rules, or identifying systemic behaviors from accumulated data. Produces validated patterns with confidence bounds and exception handling.
capabilities: Collect and structure multiple observations into analyzable datasets with quality metrics
Detect six types of patterns: frequency, correlation, sequence, cluster, trend, and threshold
Generate validated rules with confidence bounds calculated from sample size and pattern strength
Identify exceptions to patterns and quantify applicability boundaries
Provide actionable recommendations based on confidence levels and statistical validation
Track pattern stability over time and flag when rules need re-validation
author: BellaBe
securitySummary: This skill is documentation-only content providing a framework for inductive reasoning and pattern extraction. All 56 static findings are false positives triggered by YAML examples and markdown formatting. The skill contains no executable code, no network operations, no file system access, and no external commands. Safe for publication.
valueStatement: Struggling to identify recurring patterns and build predictive rules from your data? This skill guides Claude through systematic inductive reasoning to extract validated generalizations from multiple observations with confidence bounds and exception handling.
userTitle: Apply Inductive Reasoning to Detect Patterns
limitations: Requires minimum of 5 observations to generate tentative patterns, 11+ for actionable rules
Cannot perform causal analysis without additional validation through mechanism testing
Confidence levels decrease with small sample sizes and inconsistent data quality
Does not replace statistical tools for complex multivariate analysis or hypothesis testing

useCases.targetUser: Sales managers and revenue operations teams
useCases.title: Sales Pattern Analysis
useCases.description: Analyze multiple sales deals to identify patterns in deal size, cycle length, and stall points. Generate predictive rules for forecasting and resource allocation.

useCases.title: Content Performance Optimization
useCases.description: Review content performance data to detect which formats, topics, and channels drive conversions. Build evidence-based content strategies from historical patterns.

useCases.targetUser: Operations managers and process improvement teams
useCases.title: Process Improvement Discovery
useCases.description: Examine operational incidents or support tickets to find recurring failure modes and bottlenecks. Generate validated hypotheses for process optimization.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Pattern Detection
promptTemplates.prompt: Use inductive reasoning to analyze these 8 customer support tickets and identify recurring issues.
promptTemplates.scenario: Starting with a small dataset to explore potential patterns

promptTemplates.title: Validated Rule Generation
promptTemplates.prompt: Apply inductive reasoning to this dataset of 15 product launches. Generate actionable rules with confidence bounds for predicting launch success.
promptTemplates.scenario: Building predictive rules from moderate sample size with confidence metrics

promptTemplates.title: Pattern Validation and Exception Handling
promptTemplates.prompt: Use inductive reasoning to analyze these 20 hiring outcomes. Identify patterns, calculate confidence bounds, and document exceptions to the rules.
promptTemplates.scenario: Advanced analysis requiring statistical validation and exception identification

promptTemplates.title: Continuous Learning Integration
promptTemplates.prompt: Set up inductive reasoning analysis on quarterly sales data. Track pattern stability, flag new patterns above 75% confidence, and update existing rules.
promptTemplates.scenario: Implementing automated pattern detection for ongoing organizational learning

bestPractices: Start with systematic data collection ensuring at least 80% completeness across observations before pattern detection
Always calculate and report confidence bounds with sample size context rather than presenting patterns as absolute truths
Document exceptions to patterns explicitly and use them to narrow rule applicability and improve accuracy
antiPatterns: Running inductive analysis on fewer than 5 observations leads to unstable patterns with high variance
Ignoring exceptions or outliers when forming rules results in overfitted generalizations that fail on new data
Treating correlation patterns as causal relationships without mechanism validation leads to incorrect interventions

faq.question: How many observations do I need before running inductive reasoning?
faq.answer: Minimum 5 for exploratory patterns, but 11-20 observations are needed for moderate confidence actionable rules. Sample size directly affects maximum achievable confidence levels.

faq.question: What is the difference between inductive and abductive reasoning?
faq.answer: Abductive reasoning explains a single anomaly with the best hypothesis, while inductive reasoning identifies patterns across multiple instances to build general rules.

faq.question: Can I use inductive reasoning to prove causation?
faq.answer: No, inductive reasoning identifies correlations and patterns but requires separate causal validation through mechanism testing or controlled experiments to establish causation.

faq.question: How do confidence bounds work in pattern validation?
faq.answer: Confidence is calculated from sample size, pattern strength, data consistency, and recency. Bounds represent pessimistic and optimistic estimates around the base confidence score.

faq.question: What should I do when patterns have many exceptions?
faq.answer: Exception rates above 30% indicate the rule scope is too broad. Narrow the applicability by adding conditions or segment the data to find more specific patterns.

faq.question: When should I re-validate existing patterns?
faq.answer: Set expiration dates based on data volatility. Re-validate when processes change, new exceptions appear, or when adding 3+ new observations that could strengthen or invalidate the pattern.
